skill.name: slack-gif-creator
skill.description: Create animated GIFs and emoji optimized for Slack using Python animation templates, visual effects, GIF optimization, and Slack size validation helpers.
skill.summary: Create, optimize, and validate Slack-ready animated GIFs and emoji.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no malicious intent, network behavior, secret access, command execution, or prompt injection. The confirmed concerns are low risk: users run local Python examples, install image dependencies, and write/read local GIF files by chosen paths.

low_findings.title: Local Script Examples Require User Execution
low_findings.description: The skill contains many Python examples for generating frames, saving GIFs, and validating output. They are legitimate toolkit usage examples, but users should review paths and inputs before running them locally.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code blocks are direct Python usage examples, not hidden execution paths. Risk is limited to normal local execution and user-chosen file paths.

low_findings.title: Local Filesystem Read and Write Helpers
low_findings.description: GIFBuilder saves output to a caller-provided path, and validators read local GIF metadata. This is expected for a GIF creation tool, but there is no sandboxing or overwrite protection in the helper itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The implementation clearly writes GIFs and reads local file metadata. I found no network transfer, secret access, or destructive file traversal logic.

low_findings.title: Dependency Installation Command
low_findings.description: The documentation tells users to install Pillow, imageio, and numpy with pip. This is normal setup guidance, but installing packages is an external command with supply-chain considerations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and limited to dependency installation. It does not use shell interpolation or download from an unusual endpoint.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines refer to animation quality, Slack size constraints, Apache license text, or GIF optimization. No cryptographic API, hashing for trust, or weak cipher use was found at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context shows ordinary documentation, animation terminology, and license wording. There is no evidence of cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Static Network and Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are Apache License references, and the reconnaissance hits are local file size or font path checks. No outbound network request or host discovery behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Imports and reviewed code show PIL, imageio, numpy, math, random, pathlib, and local import helpers only. No requests, sockets, or subprocess usage appeared.

low_findings.title: Static C2 and Dynamic Import Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The C2 hits are variable names in easing math, and the dynamic import hit is a normal Python import shown in documentation. No command-and-control logic, plugin loader, or runtime import of untrusted modules was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The easing code uses c1, c2, and c3 constants for animation formulas. The documented import lists local frame composition helpers only.

content.user_title: Create Slack GIFs and Animated Emoji
content.value_statement: Slack GIFs often exceed size limits or look rough. This skill helps create, optimize, and validate animated GIFs for Slack.
actual_capabilities: Creates animated GIFs from PIL images or numpy frame arrays.
Optimizes GIF color count, dimensions, and duplicate frames for Slack use.
Validates GIF size, dimensions, frame count, duration, and Slack readiness.
Provides animation templates for bounce, shake, spin, slide, zoom, fade, morph, and more.
Adds visual helpers for emoji rendering, outlined text, palettes, particles, flashes, and motion effects.
Includes guidance for Slack message GIFs and strict 64KB animated emoji limits.
limitations: It does not upload GIFs to Slack or manage Slack workspace settings.
Users must install Python image dependencies before running examples.
Emoji rendering depends on fonts available on the local system.
Very detailed animations may not fit Slack animated emoji size limits.

use_cases.title: Create team reaction emoji
use_cases.description: Build compact animated emoji that fit Slack size and dimension limits.

use_cases.title: Produce announcement GIFs
use_cases.description: Create polished motion graphics for launches, milestones, and team updates.

use_cases.title: Prototype animation ideas
use_cases.description: Combine reusable motion templates and effects before refining a final GIF.

prompt_templates.title: Basic reaction emoji
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple Slack emoji GIF.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 128x128 Slack animated emoji of a surprised face pulsing twice. Keep it under 64KB if possible.

prompt_templates.title: Message celebration GIF
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a Slack message GIF with text and effects.
prompt_templates.prompt: Make a Slack message GIF that says "Launch complete" with confetti, a flash effect, and readable outlined text.

prompt_templates.title: Combined animation sequence
prompt_templates.scenario: Use multiple animation primitives in one GIF.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 480x480 GIF where a rocket slides in, shakes on impact, then zooms out with sparkles.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced optimization pass
prompt_templates.scenario: Refine an existing concept for strict Slack limits.
prompt_templates.prompt: Optimize this animated emoji concept for Slack: 12 frames, 128x128, 40 colors, simple background, and validation output.

output_examples.input: Create a tiny Slack emoji of a waving hand with a friendly bounce.
output_examples.output: A short animated emoji plan with 128x128 dimensions, limited frames, bounce motion, color reduction, and size validation steps.

output_examples.input: Make a Slack GIF for a project win announcement.
output_examples.output: A message GIF concept with outlined headline text, confetti particles, impact flash, optimized color palette, and Slack readiness checks.

output_examples.input: Reduce this GIF for Slack emoji upload.
output_examples.output: Optimization guidance that reduces frames, lowers color count, simplifies gradients, checks dimensions, and reports whether the file passes.

best_practices: Start with the correct target: 128x128 for emoji or about 480x480 for message GIFs.
Validate file size early, then reduce frames, colors, and gradients when needed.
Use outlined text and simple shapes so animations stay readable at Slack sizes.
anti_patterns: Do not create complex full-scene emoji animations when the 64KB limit matters.
Do not rely on gradients or many colors for files that must compress tightly.
Do not assume a GIF is Slack-ready without checking size and dimensions.

faq.question: Can this upload GIFs directly to Slack?
faq.answer: No. It creates and validates local GIF files, but Slack upload is a separate manual or API step.

faq.question: Can it make animated Slack emoji?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes emoji-focused dimensions, frame limits, color limits, and validation helpers.

faq.question: Why is the animated emoji size limit difficult?
faq.answer: Slack animated emoji commonly need to stay near 64KB, so frames, colors, and detail must be limited.

faq.question: What dependencies are required?
faq.answer: The toolkit uses Pillow, imageio, imageio-ffmpeg, and numpy for image creation and GIF writing.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can I combine several animation effects?
faq.answer: Yes. The templates are composable, so users can combine motion, text, particles, and validation steps.
